question:
Prove that every nonzero coefficient of the Taylor series of
\[
(1 - x + x^2)e^x
\]
about $x=0$ is a rational number whose numerator (in lowest terms) is either $1$ or a prime number.
solution:
The coefficient of $x^n$ in the Taylor series of $(1-x+x^2)e^x$ for 
$n=0,1,2$ is $1,0,\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $n\geq 3$, the coefficient of 
$x^n$ is
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{n!} - \frac{1}{(n-1)!} + \frac{1}{(n-2)!}
&= \frac{1-n+n(n-1)}{n!} \\
&= \frac{n-1}{n(n-2)!}.
\end{align*}
If $n-1$ is prime, then the lowest-terms numerator is clearly either 1 or the prime $n-1$ (and in fact the latter, since $n-1$ is relatively prime to $n$ and to $(n-2)!$).
 If $n-1$ is composite,
either it can be written as $ab$ for some $a \neq b$, in which case both $a$ and $b$ appear separately in $(n-2)!$ and so the numerator is $1$,
or $n-1 = p^2$ for some prime $p$, in which case $p$ appears in $(n-2)!$
and so the numerator is either 1 or $p$. (In the latter case, the numerator is actually 1 unless $p=2$, as in all other cases both $p$ and $2p$ appear in $(n-2)!$.)

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that every nonzero coefficient of the Taylor series of
\[
(1 - unknownx + unknownx^2)e^{unknownx}
\]
about $unknownx=0$ is a rational number whose numerator (in lowest terms) is either $1$ or a prime number.
descriptive_long.solution:
The coefficient of $unknownx^{integern}$ in the Taylor series of $(1-unknownx+unknownx^2)e^{unknownx}$ for 
$integern=0,1,2$ is $1,0,\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $integern\geq 3$, the coefficient of 
$unknownx^{integern}$ is
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{integern!} - \frac{1}{(integern-1)!} + \frac{1}{(integern-2)!}
&= \frac{1-integern+integern(integern-1)}{integern!} \\
&= \frac{integern-1}{integern(integern-2)!}.
\end{align*}
If $integern-1$ is prime, then the lowest-terms numerator is clearly either 1 or the prime $integern-1$ (and in fact the latter, since $integern-1$ is relatively prime to $integern$ and to $(integern-2)!$).
 If $integern-1$ is composite,
either it can be written as $divisora divisorb$ for some $divisora \neq divisorb$, in which case both $divisora$ and $divisorb$ appear separately in $(integern-2)!$ and so the numerator is $1$,
or $integern-1 = primep^2$ for some prime $primep$, in which case $primep$ appears in $(integern-2)!$
and so the numerator is either 1 or $primep$. (In the latter case, the numerator is actually 1 unless $primep=2$, as in all other cases both $primep$ and $2primep$ appear in $(integern-2)!$.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that every nonzero coefficient of the Taylor series of
\[
(1 - velocity + velocity^2)e^{velocity}
\]
about $velocity=0$ is a rational number whose numerator (in lowest terms) is either $1$ or a prime number.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The coefficient of $velocity^{treasury}$ in the Taylor series of $(1-velocity+velocity^2)e^{velocity}$ for 
$treasury=0,1,2$ is $1,0,\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $treasury\geq 3$, the coefficient of 
$velocity^{treasury}$ is
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{treasury!} - \frac{1}{(treasury-1)!} + \frac{1}{(treasury-2)!}
&= \frac{1-treasury+treasury(treasury-1)}{treasury!} \\
&= \frac{treasury-1}{treasury(treasury-2)!}.
\end{align*}
If $treasury-1$ is prime, then the lowest-terms numerator is clearly either 1 or the prime $treasury-1$ (and in fact the latter, since $treasury-1$ is relatively prime to $treasury$ and to $(treasury-2)!$).
 If $treasury-1$ is composite,
either it can be written as $lanterns harvests$ for some $lanterns \neq harvests$, in which case both $lanterns$ and $harvests$ appear separately in $(treasury-2)!$ and so the numerator is $1$,
or $treasury-1 = citadels^2$ for some prime $citadels$, in which case $citadels$ appears in $(treasury-2)!$
and so the numerator is either 1 or $citadels$. (In the latter case, the numerator is actually 1 unless $citadels=2$, as in all other cases both $citadels$ and $2citadels$ appear in $(treasury-2)!$.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that every nonzero coefficient of the Taylor series of
\[
(1 - fixedvalue + fixedvalue^2)e^{fixedvalue}
\]
about $fixedvalue=0$ is a rational number whose numerator (in lowest terms) is either $1$ or a prime number.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The coefficient of $fixedvalue^{continuous}$ in the Taylor series of $(1-fixedvalue+fixedvalue^2)e^{fixedvalue}$ for 
$continuous=0,1,2$ is $1,0,\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $continuous\geq 3$, the coefficient of 
$fixedvalue^{continuous}$ is
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{continuous!} - \frac{1}{(continuous-1)!} + \frac{1}{(continuous-2)!}
&= \frac{1-continuous+continuous(continuous-1)}{continuous!} \\
&= \frac{continuous-1}{continuous(continuous-2)!}.
\end{align*}
If $continuous-1$ is prime, then the lowest-terms numerator is clearly either 1 or the prime $continuous-1$ (and in fact the latter, since $continuous-1$ is relatively prime to $continuous$ and to $(continuous-2)!$).
 If $continuous-1$ is composite,
either it can be written as $multiplewholevalue$ for some $multiple \neq wholevalue$, in which case both $multiple$ and $wholevalue$ appear separately in $(continuous-2)!$ and so the numerator is $1$,
or $continuous-1 = composite^2$ for some prime $composite$, in which case $composite$ appears in $(continuous-2)!$
and so the numerator is either 1 or $composite$. (In the latter case, the numerator is actually 1 unless $composite=2$, as in all other cases both $composite$ and $2composite$ appear in $(continuous-2)!$.)

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
Prove that every nonzero coefficient of the Taylor series of
\[
(1 - qzxwvtnp + qzxwvtnp^2)e^{qzxwvtnp}
\]
about $qzxwvtnp=0$ is a rational number whose numerator (in lowest terms) is either $1$ or a prime number.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
The coefficient of $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla}$ in the Taylor series of $(1-qzxwvtnp+qzxwvtnp^2)e^{qzxwvtnp}$ for 
$hjgrksla=0,1,2$ is $1,0,\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $hjgrksla\geq 3$, the coefficient of 
$qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla}$ is
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{hjgrksla!} - \frac{1}{(hjgrksla-1)!} + \frac{1}{(hjgrksla-2)!}
&= \frac{1-hjgrksla+hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1)}{hjgrksla!} \\
&= \frac{hjgrksla-1}{hjgrksla(hjgrksla-2)!}.
\end{align*}
If $hjgrksla-1$ is prime, then the lowest-terms numerator is clearly either 1 or the prime $hjgrksla-1$ (and in fact the latter, since $hjgrksla-1$ is relatively prime to $hjgrksla$ and to $(hjgrksla-2)!$).
 If $hjgrksla-1$ is composite,
either it can be written as $vxmleqpd ncytwraf$ for some $vxmleqpd \neq ncytwraf$, in which case both $vxmleqpd$ and $ncytwraf$ appear separately in $(hjgrksla-2)!$ and so the numerator is $1$,
or $hjgrksla-1 = uglsnbke^2$ for some prime $uglsnbke$, in which case $uglsnbke$ appears in $(hjgrksla-2)!$
and so the numerator is either 1 or $uglsnbke$. (In the latter case, the numerator is actually 1 unless $uglsnbke=2$, as in all other cases both $uglsnbke$ and $2uglsnbke$ appear in $(hjgrksla-2)!$.)
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let

a)  Expand
        (1-x+x^2)ex = \sum _{n=0}^{\infty } c_n x^n
   and determine the first six coefficients c_0 , c_1 , \ldots  , c_5.

b)  For every integer n \geq  6 write the rational number c_n in lowest terms and prove that its numerator is either 1 or a (positive) prime number.
kernel_variant.solution:
Part (a)
The exponential series is ex = \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } x^m / m!.  Multiplying term-by-term by 1-x+x^2 gives
  (1-x+x^2)ex = \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } x^m / m! - \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } x^{m+1}/m! + \sum _{m=0}^{\infty } x^{m+2}/m!.
Collecting the coefficient of x^n (n \geq  2) we obtain
   c_n = 1/n! - 1/(n-1)! + 1/(n-2)! .
For n = 0,1 the coefficient is read off directly:
   c_0 = 1,  c_1 = 1 - 1 = 0.
Evaluating for n = 2,\ldots ,5:
   c_2 = 1/2! - 1/1! + 1/0! = \frac{1}{2} - 1 + 1 = \frac{1}{2},
   c_3 = 1/6 - 1/2 + 1   = 4/6 = 2/3,
   c_4 = 1/24 - 1/6 + \frac{1}{2}  = 9/24 = 3/8,
   c_5 = 1/120 - 1/24 + 1/6 = 16/120 = 2/15.
Thus
   c_0 = 1, c_1 = 0, c_2 = \frac{1}{2}, c_3 = 2/3, c_4 = 3/8, c_5 = 2/15.

Part (b)
For n \geq  2 rewrite the expression found above:
   c_n = 1/n! - 1/(n-1)! + 1/(n-2)! = (1 - n + n(n-1))/n! = (n-1)/(n (n-2)!).
Put
   d := n - 1, m := n - 2  (so that n \geq  6 \Rightarrow  m \geq  4).
Hence
   c_n = d /(n\cdot m!).
We reduce this fraction.  Because consecutive integers are coprime, gcd(d,n)=1, so every common divisor of numerator and denominator must lie in gcd(d,m!). We distinguish two cases.

Case 1: d is prime.
In this case d > m, for otherwise d would be a prime \leq  m dividing m!, contradicting d \nmid  m!.  Thus gcd(d,m!)=1 and the fraction is already in lowest terms:
   c_n = d /(n\cdot m!),
whose numerator is the prime d.

Case 2: d is composite.
Write the prime-power factorisation d=\prod _{i} p_i^{e_i}.  Because d=m+1, each p_i satisfies p_i \leq  d/2 \leq  m.  (If one of the p_i equalled m+1 it would equal d itself, contradicting the assumption that d is composite.)
For any such prime p:=p_i we compare the exponent of p in d with that in m! using Legendre's formula
   \nu _p(m!) = \lfloor m/p\rfloor  + \lfloor m/p^2\rfloor  + \lfloor m/p^3\rfloor  + \ldots  .
Since p \leq  m and m \geq  4, the first term \lfloor m/p\rfloor  is at least 1; repeating the estimate inductively shows \nu _p(m!) \geq  e_i.  Consequently every prime power dividing d also divides m!, i.e. d | m! .  Therefore gcd(d,m!) = d and after cancelling we get
   c_n = 1 /( n\cdot m!/d ),
whose numerator equals 1.

Combining Cases 1 and 2 we conclude that, for every n \geq  6, the coefficient c_n written in lowest terms has numerator 1 when n-1 is composite and has numerator n-1 when n-1 is prime.  Hence the numerator is always 1 or a (positive) prime number, completing the proof.

core_steps: Observe gcd(n−1,n)=1 and test whether prime factors of n−1 already lie in (n−2)!
Case-split on n−1 (prime vs composite; composite further to product or square) to see what survives after cancellation

slot1.description: Exact small indices handled separately before the general formula is invoked

slot2.description: Chosen cut-off for applying the closed-form coefficient

slot3.description: How the composite case is subdivided (distinct factors vs perfect square)
slot3.original: ab with a ≠ b  OR  p²

slot4.description: Explicit singling-out of the exceptional square-prime p = 2
